article_title: Unprecedented March Heat Wave Shatters Records Across the Western US as Phoenix Hits 105 Degrees

findings.message: Source redirected to different domain: edition.cnn.com

content_preview.title: Unprecedented March Heat Wave Shatters Records Across the Western US as Phoenix Hits 105 Degrees
content_preview.summary: An extraordinary heat dome is delivering summer-like temperatures across the American Southwest in mid-March, breaking century-old records and threatening already depleted snowpack.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

An extraordinary heat wave is gripping the western United States in mid-March 2026, pushing temperatures 20 to 30 degrees above normal and shattering records that have stood for decades. Phoenix recorded 105 degrees Fahrenheit on March 19, the hottest March day in the city's history, while the National Weather Service issued its first-ever winter extreme heat warning for Arizona. The event is unfolding against a backdrop of the warmest winter on record across the American West, rais...

editor_notes.source_verification: NBC News confirms 39 million under heat alerts, Phoenix 105F, heat dome description, cities at risk, snowpack drought, NWS 'one of the strongest heat domes ever seen during this time of year.' Temperature figures, geographic scope, and snowpack concerns all verified.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified: Phoenix 105F, 20-30 degrees above normal, first-ever winter extreme heat warning, 39 million affected, specific city temperatures. Rahmstorf study climate context from ScienceDaily adds legitimate scientific grounding.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Appropriately urgent without sensationalism. Climate context presented factually with proper attribution to peer-reviewed research. No editorializing about climate policy.
editor_notes.originality: Good integration of immediate weather event with broader climate acceleration research. The snowpack and water supply section adds practical context beyond the temperature records.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Solid climate/weather news at 779 words. Five sources including NBC News, NPR, ScienceDaily, and Nature. Well-balanced between event reporting and scientific context.
